source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Roo has 16 cards: 4 spades ( $(\boldsymbol{*}), 4$ clubs ( $*$ ), 4 diamonds ( $\bullet$ ) and 4 hearts $(\boldsymbol{v})$. He wants to place them in the square shown, so that every row and every column has exactly one card of each suit. The diagram shows how Roo started. How many of the 4 cards can be put in place of the question mark?
<image1>

image_dependency: question explicitly refers to 'the square shown' and asks about a card placed at a question mark location
the starting arrangement is said to be shown in a diagram, so the necessary state is not fully recoverable from text alone
asset registry shows an available image and registry_passed=true
multi_step: the solver must combine row and column constraints: each row and each column must contain exactly one of each of 4 suits
the task depends on integrating the partial visual layout with symbolic suit constraints
the question asks 'How many of the 4 cards can be put in place of the question mark?', which typically requires testing multiple candidate suits against the global constraints
this is more than direct lookup; it requires constraint propagation/elimination over the grid
verifiability: answer_type is numeric and answer_text is '2', giving a precise target
the candidate set is finite and explicit: 4 possible suits for the question-mark position
the row/column rules are exact, so correctness can be checked by deterministic constraint solving against the image-provided givens
asset registry indicates question and answer are present and registry_passed=true

risk_flags: initial card placements are image-only and not enumerated in text
verification depends on accurate parsing of the grid and suit symbols from the diagram

decision_reasons: Core assets are complete: question text, answer, and the required image are all present, and the registry passed with no reported issues.
Initial scoring is strong overall, with high image dependency, high verifiability, and solid multi-step reasoning value.
Although the initial card placements are image-only, the image asset exists and is available, so the core semantics remain recoverable.
The numeric answer and explicit finite candidate set make the sample deterministically checkable.
